name: Hildr
description: Hildr is a Valkyrie in Norse mythology, whose name means 'battle' or 'battle maid'. She is primarily known for her role in perpetuating eternal warfare. Hildr possesses the power to resurrect fallen warriors on the battlefield, ensuring that the conflict continues endlessly. This ability is most famously associated with the tale of Hedin and Högni, where she revives the slain warriors each night, prolonging their epic, never-ending battle on the island of Hoy. Her origins are somewhat obscure, with some accounts suggesting she is a daughter of Högni, further intertwining her with the cycle of violence she perpetuates. Hildr embodies the relentless nature of war and the tragic consequences of unending conflict. She is a powerful figure, capable of manipulating life and death on the battlefield, and her actions highlight the destructive potential of vengeance and the futility of perpetual fighting. While not as prominent as some other Valkyries like Brynhildr, Hildr's role in the Hedin and Högni saga cements her place as a significant figure in Norse war mythology, representing the unending cycle of violence and the power of the Valkyries to influence the fate of warriors.
subtitle: Valkyrie of Eternal Battle

keyMyths.title: The Eternal Battle of Hedin and Högni
keyMyths.description: Hildr, as Högni's daughter, attempts to reconcile the warring parties but fails, ultimately prolonging their battle eternally by resurrecting the fallen warriors each night. This ensures the cycle of violence continues indefinitely.

keyMyths.title: Hildr's Prophecy
keyMyths.description: Before the battle begins, Hildr prophesies the immense bloodshed and the unending nature of the conflict between Hedin and Högni, highlighting her knowledge of fate and the futility of their war.

keyMyths.title: Hildr's Role as a Valkyrie
keyMyths.description: As a Valkyrie, Hildr's role extends beyond simply resurrecting warriors; she also chooses who lives and dies on the battlefield, further emphasizing her power over fate and the course of the battle.

sources.author: Snorri Sturluson
sources.description: Part of the Prose Edda, containing references to Hildr and the story of Hedin and Högni.
